paragraphs: The black mulberry (Morus nigra) is native to south-western Asia and has been cultivated so long its exact wild range is unknown. Its fruit ripens to a dark purple, almost black, and is richly flavoured, with an intense sweetness balanced by a pleasant tartness that is generally regarded as the best tasting of the mulberries.
The berries are relatively large and juicy and stain readily, so they are usually eaten fresh or made into jams, pies and cordials rather than shipped.

claims.text: Morus nigra is native to south-western Asia, long cultivated
Fruit is dark purple, almost black when ripe
Richly flavoured, sweet with a slightly acidic tang, considered the best-flavoured mulberry

paragraphs: Black English is a common name for the true black mulberry (Morus nigra), native to south-west Asia (the highlands of Mesopotamia and Persia). It is one of the main black mulberry varieties grown in Australia, alongside Hicks Fancy.

Black mulberry produces the best-flavoured fruit of the mulberries, with large, juicy, dark purple to almost black berries that balance rich sweetness and tartness. It is prized for fresh eating and cooking, though the dark juice stains readily.

claims.text: Black English is a common name for the true black mulberry, Morus nigra
Morus nigra is native to south-west Asia (Mesopotamia and Persia)
Black English is one of the main M. nigra varieties grown in Australia, alongside Hicks Fancy
Black mulberry has the best-flavoured fruit of the mulberry species
The berries are large and dark purple to almost black, balancing sweetness and tartness

sources.name: SBA's Kitchen: Mulberry Hicks Fancy (Morus nigra)

paragraphs: Hicks Fancy is a black mulberry (Morus nigra) and, along with Black English, is one of the main M. nigra varieties grown in Australia. It is a smaller tree than the standard black mulberry, which makes it more manageable in suburban gardens.

It is valued for very good, consistent crops of slightly smaller but still large, luscious berries. When fully ripe the fruit is very sweet and juicy with a slight tartness, picked fresh over an extended season.

claims.text: Hicks Fancy is a Morus nigra (black mulberry)
Along with Black English it is one of the main M. nigra varieties grown in Australia
It is a smaller tree than the standard black mulberry
It bears very good crops of slightly smaller but still large berries
Ripe fruit is very sweet and juicy with a slight tartness

sources.name: SBA's Kitchen: Mulberry Hicks Fancy (Morus nigra)
Daleys Fruit: Mulberry Hicks Fancy Tree

paragraphs: King White is a name for Morus macroura, the shahtoot or king mulberry, native to Tibet and the Himalayas. It bears very long, pale fruit, several times the length of a common mulberry, that softens and becomes honey sweet when ripe.
The fruit is white to pale and described as honey sweet with low acidity and fruity notes. The long berries hang clear of the foliage, making them quick and easy to pick fresh.

claims.text: King White is Morus macroura, the shahtoot or king mulberry
Morus macroura is native to Tibet and the Himalayas
Fruit is very long, far longer than common mulberries
Ripe fruit is white or pale and honey sweet with low acidity

sources.name: Growing Fruit forum: Morus macroura King White / Shahtoot

paragraphs: Pakistan (often sold as Pakistan Black) is a long-fruited Morus macroura, selected in Islamabad and sometimes mislabelled as Morus alba. Its berries are exceptionally long, far longer than common mulberries, and ripen from red to a dark purple, almost black.

The flavour is sweet and complex, good in the red stage as well as when fully dark. The fruit is firmer than most mulberries and reported to stain less than other dark cultivars, helped by a slightly thicker skin, making it cleaner to eat fresh.

claims.text: Pakistan is a Morus macroura selected in Islamabad, sometimes mislabelled as Morus alba
The fruit is exceptionally long, far longer than common mulberries
Fruit ripens from red to dark purple, almost black
The flavour is sweet and fairly low in acid
The fruit is firmer and stains less than other dark mulberries due to a thicker skin

sources.name: Cricket Hill Garden: Morus macroura 'Pakistan' mulberry

paragraphs: Red Shahtoot is a long-fruited mulberry of the Morus macroura group (the shahtoot or king mulberries), native to Tibet and the Himalayas. Its berries are notably elongated, far longer than common mulberries, and ripen to a deep red.

Unlike the white shahtoot, the red shahtoot has red fruit that will stain. Shahtoot mulberries are described as honey sweet, with the flavour rated close to that of the black mulberry, and the long berries make for easy fresh picking.

claims.text: Red Shahtoot belongs to Morus macroura, the long shahtoot mulberries
Morus macroura is native to Tibet and the Himalayas
Fruit is very long, far longer than common mulberries
The red shahtoot has red fruit that stains, unlike the non-staining white shahtoot
Shahtoot mulberries are described as honey sweet, with flavour rated close to the black mulberry

sources.name: Gallifrey Forest Farm: Plant profile, mulberry
Daleys Fruit: Mulberry Dwarf Red Shahtoot (Morus macroura)

paragraphs: Weeping mulberry is Morus alba 'Pendula', a dwarf, weeping cultivar of the Chinese white mulberry. It is typically top-grafted onto a standard trunk, with gnarled branches that cascade to the ground, and is grown chiefly as an ornamental feature tree.

It stays small, around two to three metres, suiting courtyards and small gardens. Female trees still set sweet, blackberry-like fruit, though the weeping form is valued mainly for its sculptural, cascading habit rather than a heavy crop.

claims.text: Weeping mulberry is Morus alba 'Pendula', a dwarf weeping cultivar of the white mulberry
White mulberry is native to China
It is typically top-grafted onto a standard, with branches weeping to the ground
It is a small tree grown chiefly as an ornamental
Female trees bear sweet, blackberry-like fruit

sources.name: Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder: Morus alba 'Pendula'

paragraphs: White mulberry (Morus alba) is native to China and is the species long cultivated to feed silkworms in commercial silk production. Its fruit ranges from white to pink in cultivated plants (deep purple in the wild), unlike the dark fruit of black mulberry.

The flavour is sweet but often described as bland, with less intensity than the black or red mulberries. It is grown as much for its foliage and ornamental forms as for the fruit, which is mild and easy to eat off the tree.

claims.text: White mulberry is Morus alba, native to China
It has long been cultivated to feed silkworms in silk production
Cultivated fruit ranges white to pink (deep purple in the wild)
The flavour is sweet but often insipid, milder than black or red mulberry

sources.name: Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder: Morus alba 'Pendula'

paragraphs: White Shahtoot is a long-fruited Morus macroura, native to Tibet and the Himalayas. Its berries are genuinely white or pale pink and several times longer than a common mulberry, ripening soft and honey sweet.
Because the fruit is white or pale it will not stain, which sets it apart from the red shahtoot. The flavour is honey sweet with low acidity, best eaten fresh straight from the tree.

claims.text: White Shahtoot is Morus macroura, native to Tibet and the Himalayas
The fruit is genuinely white or pale pink and will not stain, unlike the red shahtoot
The berries are very long, several times longer than a common mulberry
The flavour is honey sweet with low acidity

sources.name: Gallifrey Forest Farm: Plant profile, mulberry
